title: Inflammatory Myopathies
authors: Steven A. Greenberg, Anthony A. Amato

items.title: Epidemiology of Inflammatory Myopathies
items.content: Understanding the incidence and prevalence of inflammatory myopathies (IMs) is crucial for clinical awareness. The overall incidence of IMs is up to 16 cases per 100,000, with a prevalence ranging from 14–32 per 100,000. Variability in diagnostic criteria across studies affects these numbers.
keyPoints: Incidence of all inflammatory myopathies combined: up to 16 cases per 100,000
Prevalence range: 14–32 per 100,000
Variability due to different diagnostic criteria and increasing recognition of specific conditions like ASyS and IMNM

items.title: Demographics and Frequency of Specific Inflammatory Myopathies
items.content: Different inflammatory myopathies have distinct demographic patterns. Dermatomyositis (DM), antisynthetase syndrome (ASyS), inclusion body myositis (IBM), and immunomediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM) occur at similar frequencies, while idiopathic polymyositis (PM) without overlap is rare. DM can affect children as juvenile DM, whereas IBM affects adults over 50 and is more common in men.
keyPoints: DM, ASyS, IBM, and IMNM occur with roughly similar frequencies
Idiopathic PM without overlap syndrome is quite rare
Juvenile DM occurs in children; IBM occurs only in adults >50 years old
DM, PM, and ASyS are more common in women; IBM is more common in men

items.title: Localizing the Site of Lesion in Myopathy
items.content: The initial step in diagnosing myopathy involves localizing the site of the lesion using history and clinical findings. Weakness can originate from various locations such as cerebral hemispheres, spinal cord, anterior horn cell, peripheral nerve, neuromuscular junction, or muscle. Clinical examination patterns like muscle atrophy, hypertrophy, scapular winging, contractures, sensory abnormalities, fasciculations, and rash help differentiate myopathies from other disorders.
keyPoints: Weakness localization involves history and clinical findings.
Patterns such as scapular winging or Gottron papules suggest specific conditions (e.g., muscular dystrophy, dermatomyositis).
Atrophy with fasciculations suggests a neurogenic process like amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Role of Laboratory Testing in Myopathy Diagnosis
items.content: When clinical examination is insufficient, laboratory tests are necessary. Serum creatine kinase (CK) is a sensitive marker for muscle destruction; levels >2000 U/L strongly indicate myopathy. Myositis-associated and specific antibodies help subtype inflammatory myopathies. Electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies (NCS) localize lesions but are less specific in determining causes.
keyPoints: Serum CK >2000 U/L is almost always indicative of a myopathy.
Myositis-associated antibodies help distinguish subtypes of inflammatory myopathies.
EMG and NCS localize the lesion site but are not definitive for cause.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Indications for Muscle Biopsy in Myopathy Diagnosis
items.content: A muscle biopsy is crucial when clinical features and laboratory tests do not definitively diagnose a myopathy. It helps distinguish between polymyositis (PM) and inclusion body myositis (IBM), especially if no characteristic rash or specific autoantibodies are present. Biopsies should be performed on clinically affected but not overly weak muscles to avoid end-stage muscle findings.
keyPoints: Biopsy is essential for distinguishing PM from IBM when clinical features are inconclusive.
Perform biopsy on muscles with MRC grade 4 strength to avoid end-stage findings.
Histologic diagnosis of IMNM can be based on anti-HMGCR or anti-SRP antibodies without a biopsy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Characteristic Clinical Features of Dermatomyositis
items.content: Dermatomyositis presents with symmetric, proximal muscle weakness and a distinctive rash. Key skin manifestations include heliotrope rash, Gottron sign/papules, V-sign, shawl sign, nail bed telangiectasias, and subcutaneous calcium deposits.
keyPoints: Symmetric, proximal greater than distal muscle weakness
Characteristic rashes: heliotrope rash, Gottron sign/papules, V-sign, shawl sign
Nail bed telangiectasias and subcutaneous calcium deposits
Skin symptoms significantly reduce life quality

items.title: Laboratory Features and Myositis-Specific Antibodies in DM
items.content: Serum CK levels are elevated in 70–80% of DM patients. Specific antibodies like anti-Mi-2, anti-TIF1-γ, anti-MDA5, anti-NXP-2, and anti-SAE have diagnostic relevance and associations with clinical features.
keyPoints: CK levels elevated in 70–80% of patients
Anti-Mi-2 antibodies: 15–20%, acute onset, good prognosis
Anti-MDA5 antibodies: 10–20% in DM, up to 65% in amyopathic DM, associated with ILD
Anti-TIF1-γ has 89% specificity for cancer-associated DM
Anti-NXP-2 and anti-SAE are linked with calcinosis, dysphagia, and higher cancer risk

items.title: Histopathology and Pathogenesis of DM
items.content: Muscle biopsy in DM often shows perifascicular atrophy. Immunohistochemical staining for MxA is sensitive and specific. Skin biopsies reveal cell-poor interface dermatitis.
keyPoints: Perifascicular atrophy present in ~50% of muscle biopsies
MxA immunostaining highly diagnostic
Skin biopsy shows cell-poor interface dermatitis

items.title: Pathogenesis of Dermatomyositis: Role of Type I Interferon Pathways
items.content: The traditional view that DM pathogenesis involves antibody-mediated endothelial cell attack has been challenged. Current evidence suggests that type I interferon pathways, particularly IFN-β, play a significant role in the microvasculopathy and tissue damage seen in DM.
keyPoints: Type I interferon (IFN)-mediated pathways, especially IFN-β, are implicated in DM pathogenesis.
Immunoglobulin deposition on endothelial cells is largely absent; complement deposition may be secondary.
Anti-Mi-2 antibodies can inhibit the CHD4/NuRD complex in nucleosomes.

items.title: Prognosis and Poor Prognostic Features of Dermatomyositis
items.content: In DM, the prognosis is generally favorable without malignancy, with 5-year survival rates between 70 to 93%. However, poor prognostic factors include increased age, interstitial lung disease (ILD), cardiac disease, and inadequate treatment.
keyPoints: 5-year survival rates for DM range from 70 to 93% in the absence of malignancy.
Poor prognostic features: increased age, associated ILD, cardiac disease, late or previous inadequate treatment.

items.title: Overlap Syndromes and Their Responsiveness to Immunotherapy
items.content: Overlap syndromes occur when an inflammatory myopathy is associated with other connective tissue diseases (CTDs) like scleroderma, MCTD, Sjögren's syndrome, SLE, or rheumatoid arthritis. These conditions are generally responsive to immunotherapies, except for Sjogren's syndrome with coexisting IBM.
keyPoints: Overlap syndromes involve inflammatory myopathies and other CTDs such as scleroderma, MCTD, Sjögren's syndrome, SLE, or rheumatoid arthritis.
These syndromes are usually responsive to immunotherapies.
Exception: Sjogren's syndrome with coexisting IBM is not typically responsive.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · OVERLAP SYNDROMES | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Immune-Mediated Necrotizing Myopathy (IMNM)
items.content: IMNM is characterized by symmetric, proximal muscle weakness with potential dysphagia, dysarthria, or myalgia. It can be associated with CTDs, cancer, or occur idiopathically. Two distinct forms are linked to anti-HMGCR and anti-SRP antibodies. Anti-HMGCR myopathy does not improve upon statin discontinuation, while anti-SRP myopathy is aggressive and refractory.
keyPoints: IMNM presents with symmetric proximal muscle weakness, dysphagia, dysarthria, or myalgia.
Associated conditions include CTDs (e.g., scleroderma, MCTD), cancer, or idiopathic cases.
Two forms: anti-HMGCR and anti-SRP antibodies are distinct markers.
CK levels in IMNM are markedly elevated (>10 × normal).
Anti-HMGCR myopathy does not improve with statin discontinuation.
Anti-SRP myopathies have a subacute, aggressive course and are refractory to treatment.
Prognosis: Anti-HMGCR myopathy responds to IVIG; anti-SRP requires aggressive immunotherapy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · OVERLAP SYNDROMES | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY

items.title: Clinical Features and Common Antibodies in Antisynthetase Syndrome (ASyS)
items.content: Antisynthetase syndrome is characterized by a constellation of symptoms including myositis, nonerosive arthritis, interstitial lung disease (ILD), Raynaud's phenomenon, mechanic hands, fever, and occasionally an erythematous rash. Muscle biopsies often show features similar to dermatomyositis (DM). The most common antibody found in patients with ASyS is anti-Jo-1.
keyPoints: ASyS includes myositis, nonerosive arthritis, ILD, Raynaud's phenomenon, mechanic hands, fever, and sometimes an erythematous rash.
Anti-Jo-1 is the most common aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase antibody in ASyS, present in 25–35% of patients with myositis.
Muscle biopsies often show histopathologic features similar to DM.

items.title: Diagnostic and Prognostic Considerations in Antisynthetase Syndrome (ASyS)
items.content: In ASyS, CK levels are typically elevated in patients with myositis. ILD can be assessed using pulmonary function tests showing reduced forced vital capacity and diffusion capacity, with spiral chest CT scans being most effective for detecting the honeycomb pattern of ILD. Histopathology reveals perimysial damage and more perifascicular muscle fiber necrosis compared to DM. Aggressive treatment, often including early rituximab, is recommended for patients with interstitial lung disease.
keyPoints: CK levels are usually elevated in ASyS patients with myositis.
ILD in ASyS shows reduced forced vital capacity and diffusion capacity on pulmonary function tests; spiral chest CT scans best demonstrate the honeycomb pattern.
Histopathology shows perimysial damage, more perifascicular muscle fiber necrosis compared to DM, and MHC-I, MHC-II (HLA-DR), MAC deposits on muscle fibers.
Aggressive treatment with early rituximab is warranted for ASyS patients with interstitial lung disease.

items.title: Distinguishing IBM from PM based on clinical features and biomarkers
items.content: IBM is characterized by slowly progressive weakness, muscle atrophy with a predilection for specific muscles, and dysphagia. It can be distinguished from polymyositis (PM) through these clinical features, CK levels, and specific blood biomarkers.
keyPoints: Age of onset usually over 50 years
Slightly more common in men than women
Weakness often asymmetric with predilection for wrist/finger flexors and quadriceps
Dysphagia can be a presenting feature
CK levels usually <10 times normal
Antibodies targeting cN-1A detected in 33% to >66% of patients, highly specific for IBM
Abnormal large granular lymphocytes on flow cytometry and reduced CD4/CD8 ratio with increased CD8 count are additional biomarkers
Mean duration from onset of symptoms to use of wheelchair or scooter is ~15 years

items.title: Diagnostic imaging and EMG findings in IBM
items.content: Muscle MRI and needle EMG can aid in diagnosing IBM by revealing characteristic patterns of muscle involvement and motor unit potentials.
keyPoints: MRI shows predilection for flexor digitorum profundus, vastus medialis, and lateralis with sparing of rectus femoris
Needle EMG may show large-amplitude, long-duration motor unit potentials

items.title: Histopathological features critical for IBM diagnosis
items.content: Muscle biopsies in IBM reveal specific inflammatory infiltrates and protein aggregations that are crucial for its differentiation from other myopathies.
keyPoints: Endomysial inflammatory infiltrates predominantly composed of CD8+ CD57+ T cells expressing KLRG1, plus macrophages
Involvement of nonnecrotic muscle fibers expressing MHC-I and MHC-II on the sarcolemma
Presence of rimmed vacuoles, COX-negative fibers, and inclusions containing beta-sheet misfolded proteins (amyloid)
Immunostaining for p62 is most sensitive for detecting these inclusions
TDP-43 extruded from myonuclein, similar to findings in neurodegenerative disorders

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.title: Initiation and Tapering of Glucocorticoids in Inflammatory Myopathies
items.content: Glucocorticoids are the first-line treatment for inflammatory myopathies, with prednisone dosed at 0.75–1.5 mg/kg up to 100 mg daily. For severe cases or comorbidities like ILD or myocarditis, intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3 days) is recommended before starting oral glucocorticoids. Tapering begins once strength normalizes or plateaus, typically over 3–6 months, reducing by 5 mg every 2–4 weeks until reaching ≤10 mg daily.
keyPoints: First-line treatment: high-dose glucocorticoids (prednisone 0.75–1.5 mg/kg up to 100 mg daily)
Intravenous methylprednisolone for severe cases or comorbidities (1 g daily for 3 days)
Tapering schedule: reduce by 5 mg every 2–4 weeks until ≤10 mg daily
Monitoring based on clinical examination, not CK levels
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.title: Use of Intravenous Immunoglobulin in Inflammatory Myopathies
items.content: IVIG is used for patients refractory to prednisone and a second-line agent, but it can also be first-line therapy in DM due to FDA approval. It's particularly effective as monotherapy in anti-HMGCR myopathy. The dosing regimen involves 2 g/kg divided over 2–5 days with monthly intervals initially, then potentially lengthened or reduced.
keyPoints: IVIG for refractory cases or first-line therapy in DM
Effective monotherapy for anti-HMGCR myopathy
Dosing: 2 g/kg over 2–5 days, repeated monthly for at least 3 months
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.title: Distinguishing Relapse from Steroid Myopathy in Inflammatory Myopathies
items.content: Relapse of myositis and steroid myopathy can be differentiated by clinical features. Steroid myopathy presents with weakness during high-dose treatment, normal CK levels, and signs like ecchymoses. Relapses occur during prednisone tapering, with rising CK levels and abnormal EMG activity.
keyPoints: Steroid myopathy: weakness on high dose, normal CK, steroid excess features
Relapse of myositis: occurs during tapering, increased CK, abnormal EMG
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.title: Methotrexate in Inflammatory Myopathies: Dosage and Transition Strategies
items.content: Methotrexate is often the first choice for second-line therapy due to its rapid action. It starts at 5 or 7.5 mg/week orally, increasing up to 25 mg/week. If no improvement occurs after a month on 25 mg/week, switch to parenteral methotrexate with dose escalation by 5 mg weekly, rarely exceeding 35 mg/week.
keyPoints: Oral methotrexate starts at 5 or 7.5 mg/week and can increase up to 25 mg/week.
Switch to parenteral (subcutaneous) if no improvement after 1 month on 25 mg/week.
Parenteral dose escalation by 5 mg weekly, rarely exceeding 35 mg/week.
Major side effects include alopecia, stomatitis, ILD, teratogenicity, oncogenicity, risk of infection, pulmonary fibrosis, and bone marrow, renal, liver toxicity.
Concomitant treatment with folate or folinic acid is recommended.

items.title: Azathioprine for Myositis: Dosage, Side Effects, and Screening
items.content: Azathioprine starts at 50 mg/d in adults, increasing by 50 mg every 2 weeks up to 2–3 mg/kg per day. It requires 6–18 months to see benefits. Approximately 12% of patients experience systemic reactions necessitating discontinuation. Screening for thiopurine methyltransferase (TPMT) deficiency is advised due to the risk of severe bone marrow toxicity.
keyPoints: Initial dose: 50 mg/d, increasing by 50 mg every 2 weeks up to 2–3 mg/kg per day.
12% of patients develop systemic reactions requiring discontinuation.
6–18 months typically required before benefits are observed.
Screen for TPMT deficiency due to risk of severe bone marrow toxicity.

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION
items.title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors and Myositis: Mechanisms, Diagnosis, and Management
items.content: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-CTLA-4, anti-PD-1, and anti-PD-L1) used in cancer treatment can lead to autoimmune neurologic complications such as myositis. Patients often experience muscle pain and weakness after one or two cycles of therapy. Myocarditis may also develop. Diagnostic features include elevated CK levels for myositis and acetylcholine receptor antibodies or nerve stimulation tests for co-occurring myasthenia gravis. Muscle biopsies reveal endomysial inflammatory infiltrates, overexpression of MHC-I on muscle fibers, and necrotic/regenerating fibers. Management involves discontinuing the checkpoint inhibitor and initiating glucocorticoids or IVIG, with gradual tapering of immunotherapy.
keyPoints: Myositis can occur after one or two cycles of immune checkpoint inhibitors
Elevated CK levels support myositis diagnosis
Acetylcholine receptor antibodies or nerve stimulation tests diagnose co-occurring myasthenia gravis
Muscle biopsies show endomysial inflammatory infiltrates, MHC-I overexpression, and necrotic/regenerating fibers
Discontinue checkpoint inhibitor; treat with glucocorticoids or IVIG
Most patients improve over several months
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION
items.title: COVID-19 and Myositis: Incidence, Pathophysiology, and Controversies
items.content: In patients hospitalized with COVID-19, 44% experience muscle pain or fatigue, and 33% have elevated CK levels. Rare cases involve myoglobinuria. Histopathology shows inflammatory cell infiltration and necrotic fibers. Autopsy studies indicate SARS-CoV-2 is not present in muscle fibers, suggesting cytokine storm as the cause of myositis. The potential for COVID-19 to induce chronic autoimmune myositis remains controversial, with temporal associations reported but causation unproven.
keyPoints: 44% of hospitalized COVID-19 patients experience muscle pain or fatigue
33% have elevated CK levels
Histopathology shows inflammatory cell infiltration and necrotic fibers
SARS-CoV-2 not found in muscle fibers, suggesting cytokine storm as cause
Controversy exists over COVID-19 inducing chronic autoimmune myositis
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION

items.title: Consider regional variations in inflammatory myopathies presentation and etiology.
items.content: Inflammatory myopathies such as polymyositis (PM) and inclusion body myositis (IBM) show different prevalence and characteristics based on geographic regions. HIV-associated PM and IBM are more common in areas with high HIV prevalence, often presenting at a younger age. Anti-HMGCR myopathy cases differ between North America/Europe and Asia regarding prior statin use association. Pyomyositis and parasitic myositis have higher incidence rates in tropical regions. Additionally, the type of cancers associated with paraneoplastic myositis varies globally; for instance, nasopharyngeal cancer is more prevalent in Asia.
keyPoints: HIV-associated PM and IBM are more common in areas endemic for HIV, often presenting at an earlier age (e.g., in the 30s).
Anti-HMGCR myopathy cases in North America/Europe are mostly associated with prior statin use; this is not true for most cases in Asia.
Pyomyositis and parasitic myositis are more common in tropical regions.
Nasopharyngeal cancer should be considered in the workup of patients from high-risk Asian regions due to its prevalence there.

items.title: Adjust diagnostic considerations for inflammatory myopathies based on regional epidemiology.
items.content: When diagnosing and managing inflammatory myopathies, clinicians must consider regional differences. In areas with high HIV rates, a higher index of suspicion is needed for HIV-associated PM and IBM, which may present earlier than typical cases. The association between anti-HMGCR myopathy and prior statin use varies by region, being less common in Asia. Tropical regions see more pyomyositis and parasitic myositis due to environmental factors. Additionally, the prevalence of specific cancers like nasopharyngeal cancer in Asia necessitates tailored diagnostic approaches for paraneoplastic myositis.
keyPoints: HIV-associated PM and IBM often present earlier (e.g., in the 30s) in high HIV endemic areas.
In North America/Europe, anti-HMGCR myopathy is usually linked to prior statin use; this link is less common in Asia.
Pyomyositis and parasitic myositis are more prevalent in tropical regions.
Nasopharyngeal cancer should be considered in Asian patients due to its higher regional prevalence.

items.question: Which inflammatory myopathy is most commonly diagnosed in individuals aged over 50?
items.explanation: Inclusion body myositis (IBM) is the most common cause of myopathy in individuals aged over 50.

items.question: Which inflammatory myopathy can occur in children?
items.explanation: Dermatomyositis (DM) can occur in children, known as juvenile DM.

items.question: Which inflammatory myopathy is more common in men?
items.explanation: Inclusion body myositis (IBM) is more common in men.

items.question: Which clinical finding strongly suggests a muscular dystrophy?
options: Scapular winging, calf hypertrophy or atrophy, and early contractures before significant weakness develops.
Fatigable weakness on examination points to a neuromuscular junction defect.
Atrophy with fasciculations suggests a neurogenic process such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
Concomitant sensory symptoms suggest a central process such as a spinal cord disorder.
items.explanation: Scapular winging, calf hypertrophy or atrophy, and early contractures before significant weakness develop are indicative of muscular dystrophy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the most sensitive laboratory marker of muscle destruction?
items.explanation: Serum creatine kinase (CK) is the most sensitive laboratory marker of muscle destruction.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In which condition are anti-3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme reductase (HMGCR) or anti-signal recognition particle (SRP) antibodies diagnostic?
items.explanation: Anti-3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme reductase (HMGCR) or anti-signal recognition particle (SRP) antibodies are diagnostic for immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM).
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition should be considered in older individuals with an elevated ESR or CRP but normal CK and EMG?
items.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica should be considered in older individuals with an elevated ESR or CRP but normal CK and EMG.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which antibody is associated with a good response to therapy and a favorable prognosis in dermatomyositis?
items.explanation: Mi-2 antibodies are typically associated with an acute onset, a florid rash, and prominent weakness but a good response to therapy and a favorable prognosis.

items.question: Which antibody is found in up to 65% of patients with clinically amyopathic dermatomyositis?
items.explanation: Anti-MDA5 antibodies are found in up to 65% of patients with clinically amyopathic dermatomyositis.

items.question: Which antibody is associated with an underlying cancer in adult DM patients?
items.explanation: Anti-TIF1-γ antibodies are associated with adult cancer-associated DM, and anti-NXP-2 and anti-SAE antibodies are also linked to underlying cancers.

items.question: What is the characteristic histopathologic abnormality on muscle biopsy in dermatomyositis?
items.explanation: The characteristic histopathologic abnormality on muscle biopsy in dermatomyositis is perifascicular atrophy.

items.question: What is the primary mechanism suggested for microvasculopathy and skin/muscle damage in Dermatomyositis?
options: Type I interferon (IFN)-mediated pathways, most likely IFN-β
Direct invasion of myofibers by inflammatory cells
items.explanation: Increasing evidence suggests that the damage in DM is primarily due to toxicity from type I interferon-mediated pathways.

items.question: Which antibodies are implicated as directly pathogenic in Dermatomyositis?
items.explanation: Anti-Mi-2 antibodies are implicated as directly pathogenic, capable of inhibiting the CHD4/NuRD complex in nucleosomes.

items.question: Which factor is NOT a poor prognostic feature for Dermatomyositis?
items.explanation: The presence of anti-Mi-2 antibodies is not listed as a poor prognostic feature for DM.

items.question: Which connective tissue disease is an exception to the responsiveness of overlap syndromes to immunotherapies?
items.explanation: Sjogren's syndrome with coexisting inclusion body myositis (IBM) is an exception to the responsiveness of overlap syndromes to immunotherapies.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · OVERLAP SYNDROMES | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY

items.question: Which autoantibody is associated with a form of immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy that does not improve when statins are discontinued?
items.explanation: Anti-HMGCR myopathy does not improve when statins are discontinued, unlike the more common 'toxic' myopathy associated with statin use.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · OVERLAP SYNDROMES | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY

items.question: Which form of immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy is typically subacute, aggressive, and relatively refractory?
items.explanation: Anti-SRP myopathies are notable for a typically subacute, aggressive, and relatively refractory course.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · OVERLAP SYNDROMES | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY

items.question: Which antibody is the most common aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase antibody in patients with myositis?
items.explanation: The passage states that the most common aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase antibody is anti-Jo-1.

items.question: What imaging modality is best at demonstrating the honeycomb pattern of interstitial lung disease (ILD) in ASyS?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that spiral chest computed tomography (CT) scans are best at demonstrating the honeycomb pattern of ILD.

items.question: What type of muscle fiber damage is more prominent in ASyS compared to dermatomyositis (DM)?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that ASyS has more perifascicular muscle fiber necrosis compared to DM, where perifascicular atrophy is more prominent.

items.question: Which muscle groups are most commonly involved early in the course of Inclusion Body Myositis (IBM)?
items.explanation: IBM commonly involves the wrist and finger flexors in the arms and quadriceps in the legs early on.

items.question: What is a highly specific diagnostic biomarker for IBM among patients with myopathy?
items.explanation: Antibodies targeting cN-1A are a highly specific diagnostic biomarker for IBM.

items.question: Which histologic feature is not always present in muscle biopsies of IBM patients?
items.explanation: Rimmed vacuoles may not be seen in as many as 20–30% of muscle biopsies.

items.question: What is the typical response to immunotherapy for IBM?
items.explanation: The myopathy is not typically responsive to immunotherapies.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.question: Which of the following is considered first-line treatment for inflammatory myopathies?
items.explanation: High-dose glucocorticoids are considered the first-line treatment for inflammatory myopathies.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.question: When is a second-line agent typically added to the treatment regimen for patients with inflammatory myopathies?
options: After significant improvement within 1 month of glucocorticoid therapy
If there's no significant improvement after 2–4 months or if low-dose prednisone tapering is not possible
items.explanation: A second-line agent is added if there's no significant improvement after 2–4 months of treatment or if the patient cannot be tapered to a low dose of prednisone.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.question: Which condition is noted for often responding to monotherapy with IVIG?
items.explanation: Anti-HMGCR myopathy often responds to monotherapy with IVIG.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.question: What is the recommended initial dose of prednisone for treating inflammatory myopathies?
items.explanation: The recommended initial dose of prednisone is 0.75–1.5 mg/kg up to 100 mg daily.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.question: Which second-line therapy for myositis is typically initiated at an oral dose of 5 or 7.5 mg/week and can be increased up to 25 mg/week?
items.explanation: Methotrexate is initiated at an oral dose of 5 or 7.5 mg/week and can be increased up to 25 mg/week.

items.question: Which drug used in the treatment of myositis requires prescreening for thiopurine methyltransferase (TPMT) deficiency due to potential severe bone marrow toxicity?
items.explanation: Azathioprine requires prescreening for TPMT deficiency due to potential severe bone marrow toxicity.

items.question: Which second-line therapy is noted for its lack of renal or hepatic toxicity and blocks purine synthesis?
items.explanation: Mycophenolate Mofetil lacks renal or hepatic toxicity and blocks purine synthesis.

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION
items.question: Which of the following is a common finding on muscle biopsy in patients with myositis associated with checkpoint inhibitors?
options: Endomysial inflammatory cell infiltrates composed of macrophages expressing PD-L1 and CD8+ lymphocytes expressing PD-1.
Presence of SARS-CoV-2 within muscle fibers.
Absence of necrotic and regenerating fibers.
Overexpression of MHC-II on sarcolemma of muscle fibers.
items.explanation: Endomysial inflammatory cell infiltrates composed of macrophages expressing PD-L1 and CD8+ lymphocytes expressing PD-1, along with overexpression of MHC-I on sarcolemma of muscle fibers, are common findings in myositis associated with checkpoint inhibitors.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION
items.question: What is a typical initial treatment approach for patients developing myositis after immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy?
options: Continuation of the immune checkpoint inhibitor without additional treatment.
Discontinuation of the immune checkpoint inhibitor and concurrent treatment with glucocorticoids or IVIG.
Immediate re-treatment with an alternative immune checkpoint inhibitor.
Use of antiviral medications to treat potential underlying infections.
items.explanation: The typical initial treatment involves discontinuing the immune checkpoint inhibitor and using concurrent treatment with glucocorticoids or IVIG.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION
items.question: What percentage of hospitalized COVID-19 patients report muscle pain or fatigue according to early series?
items.explanation: Early series reported that as many as 44% of hospitalized COVID-19 patients experience muscle pain or fatigue.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION

items.question: Which myopathy is more commonly encountered in areas endemic for HIV?
items.explanation: HIV-associated PM and IBM are more commonly encountered in areas endemic for HIV.

items.question: In which regions is nasopharyngeal cancer particularly common, influencing the assessment of paraneoplastic myositis?
items.explanation: Nasopharyngeal cancer is particularly common in Asia.

items.question: In which regions are most cases of anti-HMGCR myopathy not associated with prior statin use?
items.explanation: The majority of anti-HMGCR myopathy cases in Asia are not associated with prior statin use.

items.statement: Idiopathic polymyositis without signs of an overlap syndrome occurs with similar frequency as dermatomyositis.
items.explanation: Idiopathic PM without signs of an overlap syndrome is quite rare compared to DM.

items.statement: The incidence of inflammatory myopathies grouped together can be up to 16 cases per 100,000.
items.explanation: Epidemiologic studies suggest the incidence is up to 16 cases per 100,000.

items.statement: A muscle biopsy is always required to diagnose inflammatory myopathy.
items.explanation: A muscle biopsy is not needed if a patient has clinical features and specific autoantibodies, such as anti-HMGCR or anti-SRP antibodies.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Serum creatine kinase (CK) levels are always elevated in myopathies.
items.explanation: Not all myopathies are associated with elevated CK levels, though a markedly elevated CK is almost always due to a myopathy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Anti-MDA5 antibodies are associated with severe skin ulcerations from ischemia and rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease.
items.explanation: Anti-MDA5 antibodies are indeed linked to severe skin ulcerations due to ischemia and rapidly progressive ILD.

items.statement: CK levels are elevated in all patients with dermatomyositis.
items.explanation: Serum CK levels are elevated in only 70–80% of patients; some may have normal CK but increased serum aldolase.

items.statement: In Polymyositis, elevated CK levels are always present in uncontrolled cases.
items.explanation: CK levels are always elevated in uncontrolled PM.

items.statement: Polymyositis is a homogeneous group of disorders with consistent pathogenesis.
items.explanation: PM is described as a heterogeneous category with varied forms of pathogenesis.

items.statement: CK levels in immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy are usually less than 10 times the normal value.
items.explanation: CK levels in IMNM are markedly elevated, usually greater than 10 times the normal value.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · OVERLAP SYNDROMES | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY

items.statement: Anti-HMGCR myopathy can develop without a history of statin use.
items.explanation: Anti-HMGCR myopathy can occur in children and young adults without prior statin use.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · OVERLAP SYNDROMES | IMMUNE-MEDIATED NECROTIZING MYOPATHY

items.statement: Patients with antisynthetase syndrome have an increased risk of malignancy.
items.explanation: The passage states that there does not appear to be an increased risk of malignancy in patients with ASyS.

items.statement: Anti-Jo-1 is the most common antibody associated with antisynthetase syndrome.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that anti-Jo-1 is the most common aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase antibody in patients with myositis.

items.statement: Inclusion Body Myositis (IBM) has an increased risk of malignancy.
items.explanation: There is no known increase in risk of malignancy with IBM.

items.statement: CK levels are usually >10 times normal in patients with IBM.
items.explanation: CK levels can be normal or only slightly elevated (usually <10 times normal) in IBM.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.statement: IBM typically responds well to immunotherapy.
items.explanation: IBM does not typically respond to any known immunotherapy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.statement: IVIG can be used as first-line therapy in DM.
items.explanation: The ProDERM Trial Group found IVIG to be effective in a randomized, controlled trial in patients with DM, leading to FDA approval for use as first-line therapy.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.statement: Rituximab has been conclusively proven to be beneficial in all patients with myositis.
items.explanation: A large randomized controlled trial found no benefit of rituximab, although some authorities believe it can help certain refractory cases.

items.statement: Methotrexate is often used as a second-line treatment and can be administered parenterally if oral administration is ineffective.
items.explanation: If there's no improvement with oral methotrexate, it can be switched to weekly parenteral administration.

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION
items.statement: SARS-CoV-2 has been demonstrated in muscle fibers of patients with myositis associated with COVID-19.
items.explanation: In autopsy series, SARS-CoV-2 has not been demonstrated in muscle fibers; the myositis is attributed to cytokine storm.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION
items.statement: There are rare reports of patients with mild myositis who were able to be successfully re-treated with an immune checkpoint inhibitor.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that there have been rare reports of successful re-treatment with an immune checkpoint inhibitor in patients with mild myositis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION

items.statement: Pyomyositis is more common in the tropics.
items.explanation: Pyomyositis and parasitic myositis are clearly more common in the tropics.

items.statement: Most cases of anti-HMGCR myopathy in North America and Europe are not associated with prior statin use.
items.explanation: Most cases of anti-HMGCR myopathy in North America and Europe are associated with prior statin use.

items.assertion: A heliotrope rash combined with Gottron papules and dilated nailfold capillaries is diagnostic for dermatomyositis.
items.reason: These clinical features are characteristic of dermatomyositis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Electromyography (EMG) is specific in determining the cause of a myopathy.
items.reason: EMG and nerve conduction studies are useful in localizing the site of the lesion but less specific for determining the actual cause.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because EMG is not specific in determining the cause, while the reason correctly states its utility.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Anti-TIF1-γ antibodies are found in adult cancer-associated DM.
items.reason: Anti-TIF1-γ antibodies have an 89% specificity and 70% sensitivity for adult cancer-associated DM.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why anti-TIF1-γ is associated with adult cancer-associated DM.

items.assertion: Perifascicular atrophy is present in all patients with dermatomyositis.
items.reason: Perifascicular atrophy is the characteristic histopathologic abnormality on muscle biopsy in DM.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because perifascicular atrophy is found in only about 50% of patients, though it is a characteristic finding.

items.assertion: Inclusion Body Myositis is characterized by asymmetric muscle weakness.
items.reason: Weakness often involves the wrist and finger flexors, quadriceps, and can be asymmetric.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The presence of rimmed vacuoles is a definitive diagnostic feature in all IBM cases.
items.reason: Rimmed vacuoles may not be seen in as many as 20–30% of muscle biopsies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because rimmed vacuoles are not always present; the reason is true.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.assertion: Relapse of myositis can be distinguished from steroid myopathy by the presence of increasing serum CK levels.
items.reason: Patients experiencing relapses of myositis may have increasing serum CK levels, whereas steroid myopathy typically presents with a normal serum CK level.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES
items.assertion: Dose adjustments of prednisone are primarily based on serum CK levels.
items.reason: Serum CK levels are monitored, but dose adjustments are primarily based on the objective clinical examination and not CK levels or patients' subjective response.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because dose adjustments of prednisone are primarily based on the objective clinical examination.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · TREATMENT OF INFLAMMATORY MYOPATHIES (TABLE 377-2) | GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF SPECIFIC IMMUNOTHERAPIES

items.assertion: Methotrexate is the preferred second-line therapy for myositis due to its rapid action compared to other agents.
items.reason: Most authorities believe methotrexate works faster than other second-line agents.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why methotrexate is preferred.

items.subtopic: MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION
items.assertion: COVID-19 infection can induce a chronic autoimmune myositis.
items.reason: There have been reports of various types of inflammatory myopathies in patients following COVID-19 infection.
items.explanation: While there are reports of inflammatory myopathies post-COVID, temporal associations do not equal causation; thus, it remains controversial whether COVID-19 can induce chronic autoimmune myositis.
items.reference: Inflammatory Myopathies · MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS | MYOSITIS ASSOCIATED WITH COVID-19 INFECTION

items.assertion: The prevalence of different types of cancers varies in different parts of the world.
items.reason: This variation is an important consideration with respect to paraneoplastic myositis.
items.explanation: The statement and reason are both true, and the reason explains the assertion.
